coredata.dc:title: Uniform electrodeposition of graphene/polypyrrole conductive coatings promoted by graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Chenglong
Cui, Jincan
Sun, Yahui
Shen, Shuling
Li, Jing
coredata.dc:description: Electrodeposition of graphene reinforced polypyrrole (PPY) conductive coatings was challenging because the surface of graphene was chemically inert, leading to phase separation between graphene and PPY. Phytic acid (PA) was grafted on the surface of graphene by a two-step functionalization to enhance the dispersion stability of the electrodeposition electrolyte, as well as the interactions between functionalized graphene (PG) and PPY. The multiple phosphonic acid groups on PA molecules can dope the PPY molecules during its electro-polymerization. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) interacted with pyrrole and PG though π-π interaction, providing negative charges, which facilitated the directional movement of PG under the electric field force, therefore, promoted the uniform film-formation of the composite coatings. As a result, the composite coatings showed the electrical conductivity of 175 S cm−1，interfacial contact resistance of 8.37 mΩ cm2, corrosion current density of 0.84 μA cm−2 (by potentiodynamic polarization) and 0.95 μA cm−2 (by potentiostatic polarization), satisfying the comprehensive properties requirements for bipolar plates, benefited from the high doping level and crystallinity of PPY, the balanced functionality and electrical conductivity of PG and the promotion of film-formation by GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polypyrrole
Graphene
Electrodeposition
Bipolar plates
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrodeposition of PPY matrix composite coatings
Highly conductive graphene functionalized by phosphonic acid groups
The morphology of the PG/PPY coatings
Defects control by adding GQDs in PG/PPY coatings
Properties of the composite coatings on SS BPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics of (a) the fabrication process for the GOH and PG, (b) the electrodeposition process for the PG/PPY composite coatings.

(a) FTIR spectra, (b) Raman spectra, (c) Zeta potential, conductivity, and the dispersion stability (0.5 mg mL−1 after 48 h), (d) XPS survey spectra, and (e) high-resolution spectra for C1s and (f) P2p of graphene, GOH and PG.
SEM images from the surface of (a) PPY, (b, c) PG2/PPY, (d) PG4/PPY, (e) PG6/PPY, (f) schematic of the fabrication process of the PG/PPY coatings.

(a) PL spectra of the GQDs. Inset: Photographs of the solution of GQDs taken under visible light (left) and under 365 nm UV light (right). (b, c) XRD and FTIR spectra of CA and GQDs prepared by heating CA for 0.5 h, 1 h, and 1.5 h. (d) TEM images of GQDs prepared by 1.5 h heating of CA.

SEM images from the surface of (a) GO8/PG/PPY, (b) GQD0.5/PG/PPY, (c) GQD1/PG/PPY, (d) GQD1.5/PG/PPY, (e) GQD2/PG/PPY coatings. (f) The schematics of the deposition process for the GQD/PG/PPY coatings.
Three-dimensional morphology of (a) PG4/PPY, (b) GO8/PG/PPY and (c) GQD1.5/PG/PPY. ‘Ra’ is the surface roughness (μm).

(a) Electrical conductivity, (b) ICR values versus compaction force, (c) PDP curves, (d, e) ICR and I
corr values (PDP) of PPY, PG2/PPY, PG4/PPY, PG6/PPY, GO8/PG/PPY, GQD0.5/PG/PPY, GQD1/PG/PPY, GQD1.5/PG/PPY and GQD2/PG/PPY, (f) PSP curves of PPY, PG4/PPY, GO8/PG/PPY and GQD1.5/PG/PPY.
(a) the XPS N1s spectra, (b) XRD spectra and (c) Raman shift of PPY, PG4/PPY, GO8/PG/PPY and GQD1.5/PG/PPY coatings.
Comparison of critical properties for various electrodeposited PPY-matrix composite coatings on SS BPs.

head.ce:title: Uniform electrodeposition of graphene/polypyrrole conductive coatings promoted by graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Chemistry, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, NO. 516 Jungong Road, Shanghai 200093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Chemistry, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, NO. 516 Jungong Road, Shanghai 200093, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Nantong University, No. 9 Seyuan Road, Jiangsu 226019, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, Nantong University, No. 9 Seyuan Road, Jiangsu 226019, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrodeposition of graphene reinforced polypyrrole (PPY) conductive coatings was challenging because the surface of graphene was chemically inert, leading to phase separation between graphene and PPY. Phytic acid (PA) was grafted on the surface of graphene by a two-step functionalization to enhance the dispersion stability of the electrodeposition electrolyte, as well as the interactions between functionalized graphene (PG) and PPY. The multiple phosphonic acid groups on PA molecules can dope the PPY molecules during its electro-polymerization. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) interacted with pyrrole and PG though π-π interaction, providing negative charges, which facilitated the directional movement of PG under the electric field force, therefore, promoted the uniform film-formation of the composite coatings. As a result, the composite coatings showed the electrical conductivity of 175 S cm−1，interfacial contact resistance of 8.37 mΩ cm2, corrosion current density of 0.84 μA cm−2 (by potentiodynamic polarization) and 0.95 μA cm−2 (by potentiostatic polarization), satisfying the comprehensive properties requirements for bipolar plates, benefited from the high doping level and crystallinity of PPY, the balanced functionality and electrical conductivity of PG and the promotion of film-formation by GQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para:
The corrosion protection properties of PPY matrix composite coatings can be reinforced by secondary phase, which functioned as physical barriers. The addition of polydopamine-decorated barium titanate nanoparticles in PPY coatings reduced the I
corr from 20.6 μA cm−2 to 0.05 μA cm−2 on the surface of NiTi shape memory alloys in Ringer solution at 37 °C [7]. Graphene oxide (GO) and its derivatives exhibited exceptional physical barrier properties due to its two-dimensional geometry and high specific surface area [8–11]. The GO reinforced PPY composite coatings on stainless steel (SS) achieved a I
corr of 0.059 μA cm−2 in 0.1 M H2SO4 at room temperature, which was significantly lower than pristine PPY [9]. The incorporation of sulfonated GO in PPY coatings functioned as physical barriers, deposition templates and dopants of PPY, resulting in a I
corr of 0.18 μA cm−2 in H2SO4 solution of pH 3 at 80 °C, which was much lower than GO/PPY coatings [10]. However, these PPY matrix composite coatings emphasized the corrosion protection properties without achieving the requirement of electrical conductivity due to the nonconductive nature of the reinforcements.

Electrically conductive reinforcements, like Ti4O7, MXene and carbon materials, were employed in PPY matrix composite coatings, aiming for better comprehensive properties of coatings on BPs. The electrodeposited Ti4O7/PPY coatings reduced the ICR from 23.93 mΩ cm2 to 12.34 mΩ cm2, while the I
corr decreased from 8.34 μA cm−2 to 5.02 μA cm−2 in 0.1 M H2SO4 with 2 ppm HF solution at 25 °C [12]. The addition of MXene in electrodeposited PPY composite coatings decreased the ICR value from 108.16 mΩ cm2 to 55.24 mΩ cm2, while the I
corr reduced from 30 to 14.6 μA cm−2 in 0.1 M H2SO4 with 3 ppm F− at 65 °C [13]. Pristine graphene possessed high electrical conductivity and excellent chemical stability, which may give an opportunity to reach the corrosion protection and electrical conductivity properties of BPs. S. Liu et al. electrodeposited a graphene/PPY composite coating, which showed the ICR value of 19 mΩ cm2 and I
corr of 9.25 μA cm−2 in 0.3 M H2SO4 with 2 ppm HF solution at room temperature, lower than the ICR of 41 mΩ cm2 and I
corr of 28.1 μA cm−2 for PPY coatings [14]. Polydopamine functionalized carbon powder was also added in PPY coatings. The I
corr decreased from 49.3 μA cm−2 to 9.25 μA cm−2, while the ICR also decreased from 16 mΩ cm2 to 10 mΩ cm2, compared with the pristine PPY [15]. From the above attempts, it remained challenging to achieve the requirement of corrosion protection properties by adding the conductive particles. The main reasons lied in the following aspects: 1) the electron conduction facilitated the formation of corrosion galvanic cells, which deteriorated the corrosion protection properties; 2) the lack of surface functionality on carbon materials brought difficulty in their dispersion in electrodeposition electrolyte, resulting in defects during the electrodeposition.

The dispersion method of graphene has been studied extensively for years. Chemical functionalization of graphene was an efficient way because the surface functionality can be tailored to match the ion type, molecular structure and solvent type of targeted dispersion [16]. Surfactants were also widely used to improve the dispersion of graphene [17–20]. GO and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be regarded as special surfactants for stabilizing the aqueous dispersion of graphene with their sp2 structure and hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups [21,22]. However, chemical functionalization of graphene turned its sp2 structure into sp3, and surfactants wrapped the graphene surfaces, both of which sacrificed the electrical conductivity of graphene.

To fabricate uniform and integral graphene/PPY coatings, a two-step functionalization of graphene was carried out, introducing hydroxyl groups as the reaction sites and grafting phytic acid (PA) molecules. There are multiple phosphonic acid groups on a PA molecule, so the surface functionality of PG can be enhanced with limited reaction sites of hydroxyl groups. The phosphonic acid groups can improve the dispersion of graphene in deposition electrolytes, function as the dopant of PPY, promote the directional movement of functionalized graphene (PG) under the electric field force, chelate with metal ions as a corrosion inhibitor [16,23]. The defects in the PG/PPY coatings were further controlled by adding GQDs and GO, both of which promoted the film-formation quality. It was worth noting that the addition of GQDs would not sacrifice the electrical conductivity of PG/PPY composite coatings because the 5–7 nm size of GQDs barely affected the electron conduction.

Multilayer graphene (Xiamen Knano Graphene Technology) was dispersed in water at a concentration of 2 mg mL−1 with the addition of sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate (SDBS) by 30 min of ultrasonication (JY 96 IIN). The pH of the graphene dispersion was adjusted to 3 using hydrochloric acid. Subsequently, 0.025 g of FeCl2 was added to 500 mL of the graphene dispersion. Hydrogen peroxide (7.5 %) was then slowly injected at a rate of 250 mL h−1 using a micro-syringe pump. After a reaction period of 2 h at 35 °C, hydroxylated graphene (GOH) was obtained and filtered to remove unreacted substances [24].

The dispersion of GOH (2 mg mL−1) was then mixed with 0.1 mol L−1 PA (70 % in water) and 0.0002 mol L−1 dicyclohexylcarbodiimide. The reaction was carried out at 50 °C for 2 h to prepare PG, as shown in Fig. 1a, followed by vacuum filtration and washing.
To prepare water solution of GQDs, 2 g of citric acid (CA) was heated at 200 °C for 1.5 h, and 10 mL of deionized water was added immediately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of PPY matrix composite coatings
ce:section.ce:para: SS substrates were pretreated before electrodeposition, as described in Supplementary information (SI). The anodic deposition of PPY matrix composite coatings was carried out at a constant voltage of 1.6 V for 20 min, using the setup shown in Fig. 1b with SS anode in the middle and two Pt plates at both sides, so that the double-sided coatings can be prepared. The deposition electrolyte was the mixture of water dispersion of PG, 0.0008 mol L−1 SDBS, 0.4 mol L−1 pyrrole and 0.0126 mol L−1 benzenesulfonic acid (BSA) for PG/PPY composite coatings. The composite coatings were referred as PG2/PPY, PG4/PPY, and PG6/PPY with varying the concentration of PG as 2, 4 and 6 mg mL−1.

The GQD/PG/PPY coatings were deposited by mixing the solution of GQDs with the deposition solution of PG4/PPY. The resulting coatings were designated as GQD0.5/PG/PPY, GQD1/PG/PPY, GQD1.5/PG/PPY and GQD2/PG/PPY, respectively, when the GQDs solution was 0.5 mL, 1 mL, 1.5 mL and 2 mL. As a comparison, GO2/PG/PPY, GO4/PG/PPY, GO6/PG/PPY GO8/PG/PPY and GO10/PG/PPY coatings were also prepared by adding 0.02 mg mL−1, 0.04 mg mL−1, 0.06 mg mL−1, 0.08 mg mL−1 and 0.10 mg mL−1 GO to the deposition solution of PG4/PPY. The experimental results of GO2/PG/PPY, GO4/PG/PPY, GO6/PG/PPY and GO10/PG/PPY were presented in SI.

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Spectrum 100 PerkinElmer) was used to analyze the chemical structure of PG and GQDs in the range of 4000–500 cm−1, with a scanning resolution of 4 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos XIS SUPRA+) was employed to analyze the chemical composition of PG and investigate the doping levels of PPY. Raman spectroscopy was tested under 532 nm laser. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 ADVANCE) was used to study the structural characteristics of GQDs and composite coatings. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of GQDs were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF 600). 3D confocal microscopy was utilized to characterize the three-dimensional morphology and surface roughness of the coatings. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Quanta FEG 450) was used to examine the morphology of the coatings. The GQDs were characterized by field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Thermo Talos F200X G2).

Carbon papers (CeTech GDS 210) were used to simulate the gas diffusion layer, and the ICR of the coatings was measured using a ZY 9858 digital micro-ohmmeter as a function of pressure, applied by NL-15 manual hydraulic press. ICR value at compaction force of 140 N cm−2 was taken for comparison. The electrical conductivity of the coatings was assessed with a four-point probe (RTS-9). Polarization tests were performed using a PARSTAT 4000 electrochemical workstation in a classic three-electrode cell setup, with the working electrode having an exposed area of 1 cm2. All the tests were conducted in a H₂SO₄ solution of pH 3 at 80 °C. Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) tests were carried out from a potential range of −0.6 V to 0.6 V (Ag/AgCl) with a scan rate of 3 mV s−1. Potentiostatic polarization (PSP) tests were carried out at 0.6 V (Ag/AgCl) for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly conductive graphene functionalized by phosphonic acid groups
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2a, the FTIR spectra of GOH showed a peak at 1050 cm−1 associated with CO bonds, while PG showed peaks at 1164 cm−1, 1117 cm−1, and 1032 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of PO, PO₄3−, and P-O-C groups, respectively. The hydroxylation of graphene provided reactive sites, and multiple PO₄3− groups were grafted at each reaction site by reacting with PA. As shown by Raman spectra (Fig. 2b), the hydroxylation process caused the ID/IG ratio increased from 0.07 to 0.12 due to the introduction of structural defects in graphene, and the reaction between with hydroxyl groups and PA increased the ID/IG ratio slightly to 0.14. In accordance with the evolution of the sp2 structure, the electrical conductivity was decreased from 1000 S cm−1 to 386 S cm−1 by hydroxylation, more significant than the second step functionalization, which reduced the electrical conductivity to 294 S cm−1, as shown in Fig. 2c. Although the chemical functionalization of graphene unavoidably sacrificed its electrical conductivity, the value of 294 S cm−1 was sufficiently high as a conductive reinforcement for PPY coatings. Meanwhile, the zeta potential value changed from −25.9 mV for graphene to −36.0 mV for PG in water, suggesting that the electrostatic interparticle repulsion was improved, leading to a stable water dispersion.

The XPS survey spectra of GOH and PG were shown in Fig. 2d. After hydroxylation, the oxygen content in GOH increased from 1.73 % to 3.15 %. As depicted in Fig. 2e, the C1s peak of graphene showed that the content of CO bonds increased from 16.06 % to 28.24 % upon hydroxylation, confirming the successful introduction of hydroxyl groups. Upon the reaction between hydroxyl groups and PA, the atomic concentration of O and P was increased to 5.04 % and 0.56 % (Fig. 2d), both of which came from the grafted PA molecules. The P2p peak of PG can be deconvoluted into PO₃−-C at 134.4 eV, PO₃− at 132.5 eV, and HPO₃− at 133.2 eV. In the two-step functionalization of graphene, PA was chosen to provide as many the phosphonic acid groups with limited reaction sites of hydroxyl groups, in the efforts to keep the sp2 structure of graphene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology of the PG/PPY coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Unlike the typical cauliflower-like morphology of PPY, the PG layers were observed on the surfaces of PG/PPY coatings, as shown in Fig. 3 . The PG2/PPY coating showed nonuniform surface with PPY-dominated area, as highlighted in Fig. 3c, and PG-covered area, indicating obviously a phase-separation between PPY and PG. With increasing the PG content, the surfaces of the coatings were largely covered by PG, but defects can be seen, as indicated by the red arrows in Fig. 3d and e. As schematically shown in Fig. 3f, pyrrole in electrolyte can be attracted by PG during the electrodeposition through π-π interactions and electrostatic attractions, which neutralized the surface charges of PG and reduced the deposition driving force under the electrical field. As a result, a thin layer of PPY was deposited before the PPY-wrapped PG reached the SS anodes, so a phase separation was observed in Fig. 3b and d. Similar defects were shown on electrodeposited graphene/PPY coatings [14]. A small amount of PPY were adhered to the PG surfaces as adhesives, leaving defects on the coatings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Defects control by adding GQDs in PG/PPY coatings
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were employed as the additives in the PG/PPY coatings to control the coating defects. The PL spectrum of the GQDs aqueous dispersion exhibited excitation and emission peaks at 341 nm and 455.5 nm, respectively, confirmed its quantum effect, as shown in Fig. 4a. The XRD spectra of CA and GQDs were compared in Fig. 4b. Upon heating, the peaks of CA disappeared. The GQDs, prepared by heating CA for 1.5 h, showed a broaden (002) peak at 26.2°, indicating the formation of graphene-like structure with small crystal size. The formation process of GQDs was schematically shown in Fig. S1. The FTIR spectrum in Fig. 4c showed the characteristic peaks at 1710 cm−1, 1750 cm−1, 1180 cm−1, and 1638 cm−1, corresponding to CO, ester, CO, and CC groups, respectively. The carbonization of CA leaded to the formation of CC bonding, and the GQDs were rich in oxygen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl groups). The size of GQDs was found to be 5–7 nm, as observed by TEM (Fig. 4d).

The addition of GO and GQDs improved significantly the film-formation quality of the composite coatings, as shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. S2. Compared with the morphology of PG4/PPY coating in Fig. 3d, with increasing amount of GQDs, the coating defects were remedied gradually. Similar effects were observed by increasing content of GO (Fig. S2). GO8/PG/PPY coating and GQD1.5/PG/PPY coating showed integral coatings with smoother surfaces and less defects than the other composite coatings. GQDs and GO were chosen as the additives to control the defects in the PG/PPY coatings, because both of them had plenty of functional groups and can interact with PG and PPY by π-π interaction and electrostatic attraction, as shown in Fig. 5f. The carboxylic groups on GO and GQDs were ionized and provided negative surface charge, which can dope the PPY and drive the anodic deposition under the electric field. The addition of GO and GQDs compensated for the limited charge-carrying capacity of PG. Strong interactions between different phases were expected to solve the problem of phase separation and improve the film-formation quality.

Fig. 6 confirmed the effect of adding GO and GQDs by showing the three-dimensional morphology and surface roughness of the composite coatings. The surface roughness of the PG4/PPY coating were 3.19 μm, which was reduced to 1.45 μm and 1.48 μm by adding proper amount of GO and GQDs. Consistent with the observation by SEM in Fig. 5, the surface of GQD1.5/PG/PPY and GO8/PG/PPY coatings were much smoother than PG4/PPY coatings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of the composite coatings on SS BPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrical conductivity of PG/PPY composite coatings increased significantly with the content of PG and reached the highest value of 173 S cm−1 for PG4/PPY coatings, as shown in Fig. 7a. Compared to pristine PPY coatings, the addition of highly conductive PG improved the electrical conductivity for over 11 times. The I
corr value (PDP) of the PG/PPY coatings reduced with the PG content, as shown in Fig. 7d, due to the physical barrier effects of PG and the corrosion inhibition effects of the phosphonic acid groups. The PG4/PPY coatings showed the lowest I
corr value of 1.365 μA cm−2, which was not sufficient for corrosion protection of metallic BPs. Defects control by GO and GQDs contributed to the corrosion protection properties of coatings. All the GO/PG/PPY composite coatings exhibited better corrosion protection properties than PG4/PPY, as shown in Fig. S4, among which the GO8/PG/PPY coating had the lowest I
corr value of 0.425 μA cm−2 due to the good film-formation quality, while its electrical conductivity value reduced to 79 S cm−1 due to the electrical insulation of GO. ICR values changed in accordance with the electrical conductivity. PG4/PPY had the lowest ICR of 7.65 mΩ cm2, while the addition of GO impeded the electron conduction and increased the ICR value to 16.25 mΩ cm2.

GQDs showed similar effects with GO on defects-control of the PG/PPY coatings, as shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6. Therefore, the I
corr (PDP) was reduced to 0.838 μA cm−2 for GQD1.5/PG/PPY coatings, as shown in Fig. 5e. Compared to GO, the small size of GQDs (5–7 nm) allowed the electron tunneling, so the electrical conductivity and ICR of GQD1.5/PG/PPY remained at 175 S cm−1 and 8.37 mΩ cm2, respectively, showing a marginal difference with PG4/PPY coatings. The incorporation of GQDs in the composite coatings improved the corrosion protection properties without compromising their electrical conductivity.

To evaluate the long-term stability of the coatings in a simulated PEMFC cathode environment, PSP tests were shown in Fig. 7f. The I
corr value of the PPY coating was 8.287 μA cm−2 because the cauliflower-like morphology of the PPY coating provided pathways for the penetration of corrosive molecules [7,12,15,25,26], which was decreased to 5.117 μA cm−2 for PG4/PPY coatings due to the physical barrier and chemical inhibiting effects of PG. Surprisingly, the GQD1.5/PG/PPY coatings achieved an I
corr value of 0.952 μA cm−2, much lower than the GO8/PG/PPY coatings (3.183 μA cm−2), disagreed with the trend of I
corr values tested by PDP (Fig. 7e). A reasonable explanation was the different hydrophilicity of the GO8/PG/PPY and GQD1.5/PG/PPY coatings. During the PDP tests, the physical barrier effects of GO were predominant, leading to a low I
corr of 0.425 μA cm−2 for GO8/PG/PPY. During the PSP tests, the coatings were soaked in the electrolytes for long time and the hydrophilicity of the GO8/PG/PPY coatings favored the permeation of electrolytes. As an evidence, the contact angle of GO8/PG/PPY coatings was 114°, lower than the 123° of GQD1.5/PG/PPY coatings, as shown in Fig. S5. In addition, the adhesion strength between the coatings and the metal substrates was also consistent with the PSP results, where GQD1.5/PG/PPY coatings showed the highest pull-off strength, as shown in Fig. S6.

The GQD1.5/PG/PPY coatings showed the satisfactory comprehensive properties as the coatings on SS BPs. Table 1 compared the properties with other electrodeposited PPY-matrix composite coatings reinforced by GO or conductive fillers. Generally, GO reinforced PPY coatings focused on the corrosion protection properties without emphasizing the electrical conductivity or ICR, while the composite coatings containing conductive fillers showed poor I
corr values. The material design of GQD/PG/PPY ternary coatings took into consideration the dispersion stability of deposition electrolyte, the size effect on the electrical conductivity, the interaction between different phases, the defect control during the electrodeposition and the doping level of PPY.

The doping level of PPY can be calculated as the ratio of the -N+- peak area to the total N1s peak area of the XPS spectra [10,29], as shown in Fig. 8a. The addition of PG increased the doping level of PPY from 40.43 % to 47.68 %, because the phosphonic acid groups on PG functioned as the dopant of PPY. Similarly, the carboxyl groups on GO and GQDs increased further the doping level to 49.50 % and 50.35 %, respectively, facilitating the π-electron delocalization of PPY.

As shown in Fig. 8b, the XRD results of PPY showed the characteristic peak at 2θ = 29.80° for its (002) crystal plane. From PPY to PG4/PPY, GO8/PG/PPY, and GQD1.5/PG/PPY, the peak shifted gradually from 29.80° to 30.17°, 30.30°, and 30.41°, suggesting that the interlayer spacing decreased from 3.00 Å to 2.96 Å, 2.93 Å, and 2.91 Å. The full width at half maximum of the (002) peaks decreased from 6.40° to 5.87°, 5.65°, and 5.52°, showing the improved crystallinity of PPY. The high crystallinity of PPY can reduce the resistance of carrier migration [30,31]. Additionally, the sharp peak at 2θ = 26.2° was corresponding to the graphene (002) crystal plane, and the peak at 2θ = 15° in the GO8/PG/PPY coating was attributed to the interlayer spacing of GO.

The asymmetric CN stretching vibrations of PPY [32–34] at 1354 cm−1 was shifted to 1379 cm−1 upon the addition of PG due to π-π interactions between PPY and PG, which was further moved to 1381 cm−1 and 1382 cm−1 for GO8/PG/PPY and GQD1.5/PG/PPY, demonstrating enhanced interactions between PPY and GO or GQDs. Peaks at 930 cm−1 and 972 cm−1 were associated with bipolarons and polarons in PPY, which acted as charge carriers [35–37]. These peaks were very weak in the PPY coating, but were significantly enhanced by PG, GO and GQDs. The normalized intensities of these two peaks were increased from 0.30 and 0.34 for PG4/PPY, to 0.35 and 0.39 for GO8/PG/PPY, and further to 0.38 and 0.44 for GQD1.5/PG/PPY, corresponding to the improved electron mobility of PPY, agreed with the improved doping level in XPS results (Fig. 8a). As the schematic drawing in Fig. 5f, the addition of GQDs improved the interactions between PY and PG during the electrodeposition, leading to the high doping level, the enhanced crystallinity and the formation of polarons, as proven by Fig. 8.

A two-step functionalization of graphene was carried out to prepare PG with balanced surface functionality and electrical conductivity. The PG/PPY composite coatings were electrodeposited. The ICR of the PG4/PPY composite coatings decreased from 159.75 mΩ cm2 to 7.65 mΩ cm2, while the conductivity increased from 14 S cm−1 to 173 S cm−1, due to the high electrical conductivity of PG and improved the doping level of PPY. The defects of PG4/PPY coatings were controlled and the surface roughness was reduced by the addition of GO and GQDs because both of them enhanced the π-π and electrostatic interactions between different phases during the electrodeposition process. The I
corr values of GO8/PG/PPY were improved significantly at an expense of lower electrical conductivity and higher ICR due to the inherent insulation of GO. The small size of GQDs (5-7 nm) enabled electron tunneling and minimized the side-effect of reducing conductivity. Eventually, GQD1.5/PG/PPY coatings showed electrical conductivity of 175 S cm−1, ICR of 8.37 mΩ cm2, I
corr of 0.84 μA cm−2 (PDP) and 0.95 μA cm−2 (PSP), satisfying the comprehensive properties requirements for BPs, owning for the high conductivity and water-dispersibility of PG, the high doping level and crystallinity of PPY, physical barrier and chemical inhibiting effects of phosphonic acid groups, as well as the uniform film formation and low surface roughness of the coatings. The electrodeposition of double-sided conductive and anticorrosive coatings on SS BPs can be easily scaled-up at ambient condition because of the high efficiency compared to vacuum-based coatings and the eco-friendly process compared to solvent-based coatings.

Chenglong Gong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jincan Cui: Validation, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yahui Sun: Validation, Investigation, Conceptualization. Shuling Shen: Validation, Resources, Investigation. Jing Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the technical assistance from the Center for Instrumental Analysis of the University of Shanghai for Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.porgcoat.2025.109269.
